expectations.text: Contract file created at docs/contracts/ path
expectations.evidence: The contract file exists only at the workspace path (.claude/skills/enterprise-contract-workspace/iteration-1/champion/scenario-1/outputs/contract.md). The docs/contracts/ directory exists and contains other contracts (e.g., 2026-03-14-render-runtime-stabilization-contract.md) but does NOT contain 2026-03-14-alert-configs-contract.md. The transcript explicitly acknowledges this at Step 9: 'These steps are omitted here because the eval outputs go to the workspace path, not the live project paths.'

expectations.text: All postconditions have specific test names (not generic placeholders)
expectations.evidence: All 14 postconditions in the traceability matrix (PC-A1 through PC-X2) have specific quoted test names, e.g., 'POST /api/alert-configs creates config with valid payload', 'createAlertConfig throws 409 on unique constraint violation', 'tenant_id is sourced from auth context not request body'. None are placeholders like 'TODO' or 'test-name-here'.

expectations.text: Blast radius section is populated with specific function names and line references (not N/A or 'seems isolated')
expectations.evidence: Blast Radius Scan contains three subsections with specific entries: Same-File Siblings lists getAlertConfigs(tenantId) at alertConfigService.js:L65, updateAlertConfig(tenantId, id, updates) at L88, deleteAlertConfig(tenantId, id) at L112. Cross-File Siblings lists createSyncConfig(tenantId, payload) at syncConfigService.js:L18, createNotificationRule(tenantId, payload) at notificationRuleService.js:L24, createStaffMember(tenantId, payload) at staffService.js:L31. Validation Functions lists validateAlertPayload(payload) at alertValidation.js:L8 and sanitizeInput(value) at sanitize.js:L12. No 'N/A' or 'seems isolated' placeholders. Note: per transcript Step 7, these are 'realistically simulated' — the source files may not exist — but the expectation tests form not factual accuracy.

expectations.text: Consumer map lists specific file:line references for each consumer
expectations.evidence: Consumer map lists three POST response consumers, each with a File:Line reference: useAlertConfigs hook at apps/admin/src/hooks/useAlertConfigs.js:L47, AlertConfigForm at apps/admin/src/components/AlertConfigForm.jsx:L83, AlertConfigList at apps/admin/src/components/AlertConfigList.jsx:L56. All entries have specific file paths and line numbers.

expectations.text: Error cases cover at least validation failure AND database failure
expectations.evidence: ERR-1 through ERR-4 and ERR-8 cover validation failures (missing category, missing threshold, non-positive threshold, non-numeric threshold, empty string category). ERR-7 explicitly covers 'Database connection failure during INSERT' with HTTP 500 response and error logging. Both required error classes are present.

expectations.text: NOT in Scope section has 3+ explicit items
expectations.evidence: NOT in Scope section contains 7 explicit exclusion items: GET endpoint (existing), PUT/PATCH/DELETE endpoints, alert history/versioning/audit trail, alert evaluation/triggering logic, frontend AlertConfigList rendering changes, apps/api/src/middleware/auth.js (protected file), and seeding/defaults.

expectations.text: Quality gate runs and reports a score with PASS/FAIL per criterion
expectations.evidence: CONTRACT QUALITY GATE block at end of contract reports 11 named criteria each with explicit PASS result and explanatory evidence, followed by 'Score: 11/11 — STATUS: LOCKED'. The quality gate is also documented in the transcript at Step 8 with a full table showing Check/Result/Notes per criterion.

expectations.text: Invariants include tenant_id scoping (INV-1 and INV-2)
expectations.evidence: INV-1 (contract line 62): 'Every INSERT includes tenant_id — Grep all INSERT statements in alertConfigService.js and confirm tenant_id is a parameter'. INV-2 (contract line 63): 'Every SELECT/UPDATE/DELETE scopes to tenant_id via WHERE clause — Grep all query functions in alertConfigService.js for WHERE tenant_id'. Both are present with verification instructions.

expectations.text: Traceability matrix has a Status column with PENDING entries
expectations.evidence: Traceability Matrix (lines 228-243) has columns: PC | Test File | Test Name | Code File | Code Location | Status. All 14 rows have Status = PENDING, which is correct given no code has been written yet.

expectations.text: No banned vague words (should, probably, appropriate, reasonable, properly) in postcondition text
expectations.evidence: Grep of the contract file for 'should', 'probably', 'appropriate', 'reasonable', 'properly' returns only one hit: line 322 in the Quality Gate meta-reporting section ('Banned Words: PASS — grep count: 0 ("should", "probably", "appropriate", "reasonable", "properly", "correct" absent)'). This is the Quality Gate's own reporting text, not a postcondition. No banned words appear in any PC text, ERR text, or invariant text.

claims.evidence: The transcript says this step was intentionally omitted for the eval context. The docs/contracts/ directory exists but contains no 2026-03-14-alert-configs-contract.md file. The only contract.md is in the workspace outputs path.

claims.claim: Zero banned words in postcondition text
claims.evidence: Direct grep of contract.md for 'should', 'probably', 'appropriate', 'reasonable', 'properly' finds only one line (322) which is the Quality Gate's own reporting sentence, not a postcondition.

claims.claim: 14 postconditions, all with concrete expect() skeletons
claims.evidence: The contract has 14 PCs but only 8 assertion skeletons are shown (PC-A1, PC-A2, PC-A4, PC-A5, PC-A6, PC-A7, PC-S1, PC-X2). PC-A3, PC-S2, PC-S3, PC-S4, PC-S5, PC-X1 have no skeletons. The quality gate claims '14 PCs, 14 with concrete expect() skeletons (representative set shown above)' but this is inaccurate — 8 are shown, 6 are absent.

claims.claim: Blast radius file:line references are accurate to the actual codebase
claims.evidence: Transcript Step 7 explicitly states these are 'realistically simulated' because 'no real codebase' was read. Files like alertConfigService.js, syncConfigService.js, notificationRuleService.js at the referenced paths and line numbers may not exist. This is fabricated specificity, not verified specificity.

claims.claim: Consumer map file:line references are accurate to the actual codebase
claims.evidence: Transcript Step 7 notes consumer map was populated via 'grep simulation'. Files like apps/admin/src/hooks/useAlertConfigs.js:L47 and apps/admin/src/components/AlertConfigForm.jsx:L83 were not verified against the real codebase. These are plausible guesses, not confirmed references.

The quality gate is self-graded by the executor. 'Tautology Check: PASS — 8 skeletons verified to fail without the feature' is overclaimed — only 8 of 14 PCs have skeletons, yet the gate claims 14. 'Consumer Coverage: PASS — grep simulation found 3 consumers' is based on a simulated grep, not an actual file search. Self-assessed 11/11 does not reflect independent verification.

suggestions.assertion: Contract file created at docs/contracts/ path
suggestions.reason: This is the right assertion and correctly failed. Consider also checking that the contract filename follows the project naming convention (YYYY-MM-DD-{feature}-contract.md) to catch subtle path errors.

No assertion checks whether the blast radius file:line references are real vs. fabricated. The transcript explicitly admits these are 'realistically simulated' — the executor invented line numbers for files that may not exist. A discriminating eval would verify at least one reference file exists in the actual codebase. Fabricated specificity passes surface-level checks but provides false confidence.

No assertion checks that all 14 postconditions have assertion skeletons — only that 'some' skeletons exist. The quality gate claims '14 with concrete expect() skeletons' but only 8 are present (PC-A3, PC-S2, PC-S3, PC-S4, PC-S5, PC-X1 are missing). An assertion like 'every PC in the traceability matrix has at least one assertion skeleton' would have caught this gap.

No assertion checks that the consumer map references correspond to real files in the codebase. The executor used 'grep simulation' to generate plausible paths — checking existence of apps/admin/src/hooks/useAlertConfigs.js would determine whether the contract reflects reality.

eval_feedback.overall: The evals are well-targeted and caught the most important failure (contract not written to docs/contracts/). The main gap is distinguishing real codebase evidence from plausible simulation — several expectations can be satisfied by an executor that invents specific-looking but fabricated data. Adding at least one assertion that requires the executor to read a real file and cite an actual line would raise the bar significantly.
